s: In Egyptian mythology, the goddess Isis reassembled the dismembered body of her husband Osiris after he was murdered and cut into pieces by his brother Set.
q: Which Egyptian goddess reassembled the body of Osiris after he was murdered by Set?
e: Set killed Osiris and scattered his body parts across Egypt. Isis searched for and found every piece except one, then used her magic to resurrect Osiris long enough to conceive their son Horus. Osiris then became ruler of the underworld.

s: The Japanese oni are fearsome ogre-like demons typically depicted with red or blue skin, horns, wild hair, and carrying iron clubs called kanabō.
q: In Japanese folklore, what are the fearsome horned, club-wielding demons called?
e: Oni are among the most recognizable yokai in Japanese folklore. They are associated with disease, disaster, and punishment. In the story of Momotaro (Peach Boy), the hero travels to Onigashima (Demon Island) to defeat them.

s: In Greek mythology, the Minotaur was a creature with the body of a man and the head of a bull, imprisoned in the Labyrinth of Crete built by the inventor Daedalus.
q: What structure did Daedalus build to imprison the Minotaur in Greek mythology?
e: The Minotaur was born from Queen Pasiphae of Crete and a divine bull. King Minos ordered Daedalus to build the Labyrinth to contain it. The hero Theseus eventually slew the Minotaur with help from Ariadne's thread.

s: Loki, the Norse trickster god, is the father of several monstrous beings including Fenrir the giant wolf, Jörmungandr the world serpent, and Hel, ruler of the dead.
q: In Norse mythology, which trickster god is the father of Fenrir, Jörmungandr, and Hel?
e: Loki fathered these beings with the giantess Angrboða. The gods feared these offspring so much that Odin cast Hel to the underworld, threw Jörmungandr into the ocean, and bound Fenrir with a magical chain.

s: The Celtic festival of Samhain, celebrated around October 31st, marked the end of the harvest season and was believed to be a time when the boundary between the living and dead grew thin.
q: What ancient Celtic festival, celebrated around October 31st, is considered a precursor to Halloween?
e: Samhain is an ancient Gaelic festival that influenced modern Halloween. The Celts believed spirits could cross into the mortal world during Samhain, and they lit bonfires and wore costumes to ward off ghosts.

s: In Polynesian mythology, the demigod Māui fished up the North Island of New Zealand from the ocean using a magical fishhook made from his grandmother's jawbone.
q: Which Polynesian demigod is said to have fished the North Island of New Zealand out of the ocean?
e: Māui is a culture hero across Polynesian traditions. In Māori mythology, the North Island of New Zealand is called Te Ika-a-Māui (The Fish of Māui), and its shape is said to resemble a giant stingray that Māui hauled from the sea.

s: Anansi the Spider is a trickster figure in West African and Caribbean folklore who uses cunning and wit rather than strength to outsmart more powerful opponents.
q: What West African and Caribbean trickster figure takes the form of a spider and uses wit to defeat stronger opponents?
e: Anansi originates from the Akan people of Ghana and was carried to the Caribbean through the Atlantic slave trade. Anansi stories often teach lessons about intelligence triumphing over brute force and are foundational to oral storytelling traditions.

s: In Slavic mythology, Baba Yaga is a fearsome witch who lives in a hut that stands on giant chicken legs and can move through the forest on its own.
q: In Slavic folklore, what is unusual about the witch Baba Yaga's hut?
a: It stands on giant chicken legs
d: It stands on giant goat legs
It floats on water like a boat
It swims through the air like water
e: Baba Yaga is one of the most iconic figures in Slavic folklore. She can be either helpful or harmful depending on the story. Her hut spins around and is surrounded by a fence made of human bones topped with glowing skulls.

s: The Aboriginal Australian Dreamtime (or Dreaming) describes the time of creation when ancestral spirit beings shaped the land, animals, plants, and laws that govern life.
q: What do Aboriginal Australians call the time of creation when ancestral spirits shaped the world?
e: Dreamtime is not simply a past era but an ongoing spiritual reality in Aboriginal belief. The ancestral beings traveled across a formless land, creating rivers, mountains, and all living things through their actions, songs, and dreams.

s: In Mesopotamian mythology, Gilgamesh was a legendary king of Uruk who undertook an epic quest for immortality after the death of his friend Enkidu, making the Epic of Gilgamesh one of the oldest known works of literature.
q: What ancient Mesopotamian king undertook an epic quest for immortality, featured in one of the oldest works of literature?
e: The Epic of Gilgamesh dates to around 2100 BCE in Sumerian tradition. Gilgamesh was two-thirds divine and one-third human. Despite his quest, he ultimately failed to achieve immortality, learning that lasting fame through great deeds is humanity's true legacy.

s: In Chinese mythology, the Jade Emperor is the supreme ruler of heaven and all realms of existence, presiding over a celestial bureaucracy that mirrors earthly government.
q: Who is the supreme ruler of heaven in Chinese mythology, presiding over a celestial bureaucracy?
e: The Jade Emperor (Yù Huáng) is the most important deity in Chinese folk religion and Taoism. He governs through a vast heavenly administration of gods, each assigned specific duties, reflecting the Chinese imperial bureaucratic system.

s: In Korean mythology, Dangun is the legendary founder of Gojoseon, the first Korean kingdom, said to be the grandson of the Lord of Heaven who descended to earth on Mount Baekdu.
q: Who is the legendary founder of the first Korean kingdom, Gojoseon, in Korean mythology?
e: The Dangun creation myth tells of Hwanung descending from heaven and a bear who transformed into a woman after enduring trials. Their son Dangun founded Gojoseon in 2333 BCE, a date still celebrated in Korea as National Foundation Day.

s: King Arthur's sword Excalibur was, in most legends, given to him by the Lady of the Lake, a mystical figure whose arm rose from the water holding the enchanted blade.
q: In Arthurian legend, who gave King Arthur the sword Excalibur?
e: While the Sword in the Stone is a separate weapon in many versions, Excalibur was received from the Lady of the Lake. Its scabbard was said to be even more valuable, as it prevented the wearer from bleeding from any wound.

s: The original Brothers Grimm version of Cinderella had no fairy godmother — instead, a magical tree growing on her mother's grave provided the dresses, and the stepsisters cut off parts of their feet to fit the glass slipper.
q: In the original Brothers Grimm version of Cinderella, what provided her with magical dresses instead of a fairy godmother?
a: A tree growing on her mother's grave
e: The Grimm version, called 'Aschenputtel,' is far darker than the Disney adaptation. Birds served as Cinderella's helpers, and the stepsisters mutilated their feet. At the wedding, doves pecked out the stepsisters' eyes as punishment.

s: Coyote is a central trickster figure in many Native American mythologies, sometimes creating the world, sometimes causing chaos, and often teaching lessons through mischief.
q: Which animal serves as a central trickster figure across many Native American mythologies?
e: Coyote appears across numerous Native American traditions from the Plains to the Pacific Northwest. Unlike purely evil figures, Coyote is morally ambiguous — a creator who also brings disorder, reflecting the complexity of the natural world.

s: In Hindu mythology, Vishnu's avatar Narasimha appeared as a half-man, half-lion to defeat the demon king Hiranyakashipu, who had a boon making him nearly invincible.
q: Which avatar of Vishnu, appearing as half-man and half-lion, defeated the demon Hiranyakashipu?
e: Hiranyakashipu could not be killed by man or animal, inside or outside, by day or night, on earth or in sky. Narasimha circumvented all conditions by being half-man half-lion, killing him at twilight on a threshold across his lap.

s: The Greek myth of Orpheus tells of a musician so talented that his lyre playing could charm animals, trees, and even rocks, and he descended to the underworld to retrieve his dead wife Eurydice.
q: In Greek mythology, which legendary musician descended to the underworld to bring back his wife Eurydice?
e: Orpheus's music was so beautiful that Hades agreed to release Eurydice on one condition: Orpheus must not look back at her until they reached the surface. Tragically, he looked back just before reaching daylight, losing her forever.

s: In Chinese mythology, Sun Wukong (the Monkey King) was born from a magical stone, learned supernatural powers, and caused havoc in heaven before being trapped under a mountain by the Buddha for 500 years.
q: In Chinese mythology, which character was born from stone and imprisoned under a mountain by the Buddha for 500 years?
e: Sun Wukong is the protagonist of the classic novel Journey to the West. After his imprisonment, he was freed to serve as protector of the monk Xuanzang on a pilgrimage to India to retrieve Buddhist scriptures.

s: In Yoruba mythology, Ogun is the god of iron, war, and labor, and is considered the patron of blacksmiths, warriors, and all who work with metal.
q: Which Yoruba deity is the god of iron, war, and labor, and patron of blacksmiths?
e: Ogun is one of the most important Orisha in the Yoruba tradition. He is credited with clearing the path for other gods to descend to earth using his iron machete. He is honored in both African and diaspora religions like Candomblé and Vodou.

s: In Slavic mythology, the Firebird (Zhar-Ptitsa) is a magical glowing bird whose feathers shine like gold and flame, and obtaining one of its feathers often begins a hero's perilous quest.
q: In Slavic folklore, what magical creature has glowing golden feathers that often set a hero on a quest?
e: The Firebird is a recurring figure in Russian fairy tales. Its feathers continue to glow even after being plucked. Heroes who find a feather are typically warned not to pick it up, but doing so draws them into an adventure filled with magical tasks.

s: In Polynesian mythology, the god Tāne separated his parents Rangi (Sky Father) and Papa (Earth Mother) by pushing them apart, allowing light to enter the world for the first time.
q: In Polynesian mythology, which god separated Sky Father and Earth Mother to bring light into the world?
e: Before their separation, Rangi and Papa clung together in darkness, trapping their children between them. Tāne lay on his back and pushed Rangi upward with his legs, creating the sky and letting light flood the earth. His brothers became gods of different domains.

s: In Mesopotamian mythology, Inanna (Ishtar) descended to the underworld and had to pass through seven gates, removing one piece of divine regalia at each gate until she arrived naked and powerless.
q: How many gates did the Mesopotamian goddess Inanna pass through during her descent to the underworld?
e: Inanna's descent is one of the oldest recorded myths. She went to visit her sister Ereshkigal, queen of the dead. At each gate she was stripped of her powers. She was killed but later resurrected, though she had to find a substitute to take her place.

s: In Arthurian legend, the wizard Merlin was imprisoned forever by the enchantress Nimue (the Lady of the Lake), who used his own magic against him to seal him in a crystal cave or tree.
q: In Arthurian legend, who imprisoned the wizard Merlin using his own magic?
e: Merlin had taught Nimue his most powerful spells, and she used them to trap him permanently. Different versions place him in a crystal cave, a hollow oak tree, or beneath a stone. Despite his prophetic powers, Merlin could not prevent his own entrapment.

s: The Norse god Thor's hammer Mjölnir was crafted by the dwarf brothers Sindri and Brokkr, but Loki's interference during its forging caused the handle to be shorter than intended.
q: In Norse mythology, why does Thor's hammer Mjölnir have an unusually short handle?
a: Loki interfered during its forging by the dwarves
d: The giant Thrym stole it during its creation
Loki accidentally melted it in the furnace
A curse placed by the gods shortened it
Sindri intentionally made it short for balance
An accident during transport broke the handle
e: Loki had wagered his head that the dwarves could not make gifts finer than those made by the Sons of Ivaldi. While Brokkr worked the bellows, Loki transformed into a fly and bit him, causing Sindri to make Mjölnir's handle too short. The gods still judged it the finest gift.

s: In the original Little Red Riding Hood by Charles Perrault (1697), there is no woodcutter rescue — the wolf simply eats the girl and the story ends as a cautionary moral tale.
q: In Charles Perrault's original 1697 version of Little Red Riding Hood, what happens at the end?
a: The wolf eats her and the story ends
d: A huntsman rescues her from the wolf
She outwits the wolf and escapes
Her mother saves her at the last moment
A woodcutter cuts the wolf open
She befriends the wolf and returns home
The wolf transforms back into a man
e: Perrault's version ends with the wolf devouring Little Red Riding Hood. The moral explicitly warns young women about charming strangers. The rescue by a woodcutter was added later by the Brothers Grimm in their 1812 version.

s: In Egyptian mythology, Ra sailed through the underworld (Duat) each night in a boat, battling the chaos serpent Apophis, and emerged each dawn as the rising sun.
q: In Egyptian mythology, which chaos serpent did the sun god Ra battle each night in the underworld?
e: The Egyptians believed the sun's daily cycle was Ra's journey across the sky by day and through the underworld by night. Each night, Apophis tried to swallow Ra and prevent dawn. Other gods helped Ra defeat the serpent to ensure the sun would rise again.

s: Quetzalcoatl, the Feathered Serpent, was one of the most important deities in Mesoamerican mythology, associated with wind, air, learning, and the morning star Venus.
q: What is the name of the Feathered Serpent deity in Mesoamerican mythology, associated with wind and learning?
e: Quetzalcoatl was worshipped by the Aztecs, Maya (as Kukulkan), and earlier civilizations. As a god of knowledge, he was said to have given humans corn, the calendar, and books. His name combines quetzal (a bird with precious feathers) and coatl (serpent).

s: The Hindu goddess Kali is depicted with blue-black skin, a necklace of severed heads, and a skirt of severed arms, yet she is also worshipped as a protective mother goddess who destroys evil.
q: Which Hindu goddess, depicted with a necklace of heads, is paradoxically worshipped as a protective mother who destroys evil?
e: Kali emerged from the goddess Durga during battle against demons. Her fearsome appearance represents the destruction of ego and evil. Despite her terrifying form, she is deeply beloved in Hinduism, especially in Bengal, as a compassionate mother who protects her devotees.

s: In Japanese mythology, Amaterasu is the sun goddess who once hid in a cave, plunging the world into darkness, and was lured out only when the other gods performed a riotous dance outside.
q: In Japanese mythology, which sun goddess hid in a cave and plunged the world into darkness?
e: Amaterasu retreated to the Ama-no-Iwato cave after a conflict with her brother Susanoo. The goddess Ame-no-Uzume performed a comedic, scandalous dance that made the other gods laugh so loudly that Amaterasu peeked out in curiosity, restoring sunlight to the world.

s: In Greek mythology, Athena was born fully grown and armored from the forehead of Zeus after he swallowed her pregnant mother Metis.
q: In Greek mythology, from where did the goddess Athena spring forth fully grown and armored?
e: Zeus swallowed the Titaness Metis because of a prophecy that her children would be more powerful than their father. He later developed a terrible headache, and Hephaestus split his skull open, from which Athena emerged fully armed with a war cry.

s: The Wendigo is a creature from Algonquian Native American folklore — a gaunt, cannibalistic spirit associated with winter, famine, and the dangers of insatiable greed.
q: In Algonquian Native American folklore, what cannibalistic spirit is associated with winter famine and insatiable greed?
e: In Algonquian belief, a person could become a Wendigo through cannibalism or extreme greed. The Wendigo is perpetually starving despite consuming everything it finds, growing larger with each meal. The legend served as a warning against selfishness and overconsumption.

s: In Norse mythology, Odin sacrificed one of his eyes at Mímir's Well in exchange for cosmic wisdom, which is why he is often depicted as one-eyed.
q: In Norse mythology, what did Odin sacrifice in exchange for wisdom from Mímir's Well?
e: Mímir's Well lay beneath one of the roots of Yggdrasil and contained water of profound wisdom. Odin willingly gave up his eye as the price for drinking from it. This sacrifice reflects the Norse value of gaining knowledge through personal sacrifice.

s: In African Bushongo (Kuba) mythology, the creator god Bumba vomited the sun, moon, stars, and then various animals into existence, including the leopard, eagle, and fish.
q: In Bushongo mythology, how did the creator god Bumba bring the world into existence?
a: By vomiting the sun, moon, stars, and animals
e: The Bushongo creation myth from Central Africa describes Bumba as existing alone in darkness and water. Seized by stomach pain, he vomited up the sun (which dried the water to make land), the moon, stars, and nine living creatures that created all other animals.

s: The original Sleeping Beauty tale by Giambattista Basile (1634) features a king who assaults the sleeping princess, and she only awakens when one of the resulting twins sucks a splinter from her finger.
q: In the earliest known version of Sleeping Beauty (by Basile, 1634), how does the princess finally awaken?
a: A newborn twin sucks the enchanted splinter from her finger
e: Basile's 'Sun, Moon, and Talia' is far darker than later versions. The princess is not awakened by a kiss but by her newborn child accidentally removing the enchanted splinter. The story was softened considerably by Perrault and later by Disney.

s: In Chinese mythology, Pangu hatched from a cosmic egg and spent 18,000 years pushing the sky and earth apart, and when he died, his body parts became features of the natural world.
q: In Chinese mythology, which primordial being hatched from a cosmic egg and separated sky from earth?
e: Pangu is the first living being in Chinese creation mythology. His breath became wind and clouds, his voice became thunder, his left eye the sun and right eye the moon, his blood the rivers, and his hair the stars and vegetation.

s: In Greek mythology, Sisyphus was condemned to push a boulder up a hill for eternity, only for it to roll back down each time he neared the top, as punishment for his deceit of the gods.
q: In Greek mythology, who was condemned to push a boulder up a hill for eternity?
e: Sisyphus was a king of Corinth who cheated death twice by tricking both Thanatos and Persephone. Zeus punished him with endless futile labor in the underworld. The philosopher Albert Camus later used Sisyphus as a metaphor for the absurd condition of human existence.

s: In Hindu mythology, the Ramayana tells of Prince Rama's quest to rescue his wife Sita from the demon king Ravana, aided by the monkey god Hanuman and his army.
q: In the Hindu epic Ramayana, who kidnapped Prince Rama's wife Sita?
e: The Ramayana is one of the two great Sanskrit epics of India. Ravana, the ten-headed king of Lanka, kidnapped Sita. Hanuman, the devoted monkey warrior, helped Rama build a bridge to Lanka and defeat Ravana. The epic explores duty, devotion, and righteousness.

s: In Celtic mythology, the Tuatha Dé Danann were a supernatural race of god-like beings who ruled Ireland before being driven underground to become the fairy folk (Aos Sí).
q: In Celtic mythology, which supernatural race ruled Ireland before retreating underground to become the fairy folk?
e: The Tuatha Dé Danann were skilled in magic, science, and art. When defeated by the Milesians (ancestors of the Irish), they retreated into the sídhe (fairy mounds). Over time, they evolved in folklore into the fairies and mythical beings of Irish tradition.

s: In Norse mythology, Odin hung himself from Yggdrasil for nine days and nights, pierced by his own spear, in order to gain knowledge of the runes.
q: In Norse mythology, how did Odin gain knowledge of the runes?
a: By hanging himself from Yggdrasil for nine days
e: This act of self-sacrifice is described in the poem Hávamál. Odin received no food or water during his ordeal. At the end, the runes revealed themselves to him, giving him great magical knowledge. This reflects the Norse belief that wisdom requires sacrifice.

s: In Aboriginal Australian mythology, the Rainbow Serpent is one of the most important creator beings, shaping the landscape by slithering across the formless earth and creating rivers, mountains, and valleys.
q: In Aboriginal Australian mythology, what creator being shaped the landscape by slithering across the earth?
e: The Rainbow Serpent appears across many Aboriginal cultures with different names. It is associated with water, fertility, and life. It can be benevolent or destructive and is often linked to the rainbow that appears after rain. It remains a living spiritual belief.

s: In Korean mythology, the dokkaebi are mischievous goblin-like spirits that carry magical clubs (bangmangi) that can summon objects and are known for their love of riddles and wrestling.
q: In Korean folklore, what mischievous goblin-like spirits carry magical clubs and love wrestling?
e: Dokkaebi are unique Korean supernatural beings, distinct from the Japanese oni despite visual similarities. They are formed from discarded objects imbued with spiritual energy. Their magical clubs can summon anything, and they challenge travelers to wrestling matches.

s: Raven is a creator and trickster figure in Pacific Northwest Native American mythology, credited with releasing the sun from a box and bringing light to the world.
q: In Pacific Northwest Native American mythology, which trickster released the sun from a box to bring light to the world?
e: In Tlingit, Haida, and other Pacific Northwest traditions, Raven found the world in darkness and tricked a chief who kept the sun hidden in a box. Raven transformed into a baby, was adopted by the chief, and then opened the box to release sunlight.

s: The Greek hero Heracles (Hercules) had to complete twelve seemingly impossible labors as penance, including slaying the nine-headed Hydra and capturing Cerberus, the three-headed guard dog of the underworld.
q: How many labors did the Greek hero Heracles have to complete as penance?
e: The Twelve Labors were assigned by King Eurystheus. Each task seemed impossible: the Hydra grew two heads for each one cut off, the Nemean Lion's skin was impervious to weapons, and Cerberus guarded the entrance to Hades. Heracles completed them all.

s: In Chinese mythology, Nüwa is the goddess who created humanity from yellow clay and later repaired the sky after the water god Gong Gong smashed one of the pillars holding it up.
q: In Chinese mythology, which goddess created humanity from clay and later repaired the broken sky?
e: Nüwa shaped the first humans from clay, using a rope dipped in mud for common people and hand-sculpting nobles. When a great flood caused the sky to crack, she smelted five-colored stones to patch the firmament and used a giant turtle's legs as pillars.

s: In Greek mythology, the phoenix is a mythical bird that cyclically dies by bursting into flames and is reborn from its own ashes, symbolizing immortality and renewal.
q: What mythical bird dies in flames and is reborn from its own ashes?
e: The phoenix legend likely originated in ancient Egypt (as the Bennu bird) and was adopted by the Greeks. Only one phoenix exists at a time, and it lives for 500 to 1,000 years. Its tears are said to have healing properties.

s: Eshu (also called Elegba or Legba) is a trickster deity in Yoruba and diaspora religions who guards crossroads and serves as the messenger between humans and the gods.
q: Which Yoruba trickster deity guards crossroads and serves as messenger between humans and gods?
e: Eshu is not evil despite his trickster nature — he ensures that sacrifices reach the gods and tests human character. No ritual can begin without first honoring Eshu, as he controls the pathways of communication between the mortal and divine realms.

s: In Japanese folklore, the Kappa is a water imp that inhabits rivers and ponds, has a dish-like depression on its head filled with water, and can be defeated by bowing — since it must bow back, spilling its power.
q: In Japanese folklore, how can a Kappa water imp be defeated?
a: By bowing to it, causing it to bow back and spill its head-water
d: By striking it with a sword
By trapping it in a cage
By challenging it to a duel
e: Kappa are one of Japan's most well-known yokai. They are said to drag people and horses underwater. The water in the dish (sara) on their head is the source of their power. If tricked into bowing, the water spills and they become helpless.

s: In Norse mythology, Freya is the goddess of love, beauty, and war who rides a chariot pulled by two large cats and wears a cloak of falcon feathers that allows her to fly.
q: In Norse mythology, which goddess rides a chariot pulled by two cats and receives half of the battle-slain?
e: Freya is one of the most important Vanir gods. She receives half of the warriors slain in battle (the other half go to Odin's Valhalla). She also possesses the Brísingamen, a magnificent necklace crafted by dwarves, and practices the magic called seiðr.

s: Robin Hood is an English folk hero who, according to legend, robbed from the rich and gave to the poor while living as an outlaw in Sherwood Forest with his band of Merry Men.
q: Which English folk hero lived in Sherwood Forest and robbed from the rich to give to the poor?
e: Robin Hood first appeared in medieval ballads from the 13th-14th centuries. His companions included Little John, Friar Tuck, and Maid Marian. While his historical existence is debated, he became a powerful symbol of resistance against unjust authority.

s: In Egyptian mythology, the goddess Ma'at represented truth, justice, and cosmic order, and her feather was used to weigh the hearts of the dead in the judgment of the afterlife.
q: Whose feather was used to weigh the hearts of the dead in Egyptian afterlife judgment?
e: In the Hall of Two Truths, Anubis weighed the heart of the deceased against Ma'at's feather of truth. If the heart was lighter than the feather, the soul could enter the afterlife. If heavier with sin, the monster Ammit devoured it.
